set.analysis_note: Nakedness before each other produced no inward shame; the absence of shame marks a state of inner transparency and relational wholeness with no guilt or self-consciousness.
The term here carries the sense of loyal, life-saving kindness shown by God to Lot; the verse does not evidence shame-cluster inner content but rather covenantal favor and mercy.
Innocence is located explicitly in the hands and the heart together, indicating a clean inner will and blameless outward action—Abimelech claims his conscience and conduct are both untainted.

The term (bluntness/cleanness of hands) pairs with heart-integrity, locating moral purity in the practical faculty of action; the hands' cleanness reflects an inner state free of deliberate wrongdoing.
The term here denotes loyal faithfulness expected between spouses in hardship; no shame-cluster inner-being content is evidenced—this is relational obligation, not disgrace.
Steadfast kindness is presented as a mutual relational commitment sworn before God; the verse frames loyal dealing as a moral obligation binding the will, not as shame.
Steadfast love is appealed to as God's disposition of loyal favor toward Abraham; it operates as a divine inner characteristic directing providential action, not as shame content.
Steadfast love is understood as God's behind-the-scenes appointment of circumstances; the servant reads providential events as evidence of God's loyal inner commitment to his master.
Steadfast love and faithfulness are celebrated as God's enduring inner commitments that have not been withdrawn; gratitude recognizes these as the motivating source of providential guidance.
Steadfast love and faithfulness are presented as qualities of character expected to govern relational decision-making; the verse frames them as moral obligations that should direct the will.
Jacob confesses unworthiness to receive God's steadfast love, acknowledging that God's loyal faithfulness vastly exceeds what he deserves; it functions as unmerited, grace-laden commitment.
God's steadfast love is shown actively toward Joseph in prison, producing favor in others' eyes; it is a divine inner disposition that transforms outer circumstances for the vulnerable.
Joseph requests a specific act of loyal kindness—intercession before Pharaoh—framing it as a relational obligation owed to him; no shame inner-being content is present.
Jacob asks Joseph to pledge kindness and truth in burying him properly; the term names a loyal, dutiful act owed within family bonds, directed by the will toward honoring the dying.
God's steadfast love is the inner motivating force behind his redemptive leading of Israel; it is the disposition from which rescue and guidance flow, not a shame term in this context.
God's steadfast love is directed toward thousands who love and obey him; it is a responsive divine commitment engaged by human loyalty, not shame content.
Steadfast love is proclaimed as a core attribute of God's character alongside mercy and faithfulness; it names an enduring inner disposition of loyal favor that defines who God is.

God's steadfast love is kept actively for thousands while he simultaneously holds the guilty accountable; it coexists with justice, showing it as a sustained inner commitment, not unconditional permissiveness.

Being shamed (humiliated) is depicted as a consequence of parental displeasure severe enough to require social separation; the inner impact is dishonor felt publicly and embodied through exclusion from community.
Steadfast love is an abounding divine attribute paired with patience; it enables forgiveness of iniquity while not nullifying justice, presenting it as a generous, enduring inner commitment.
Moses appeals to the greatness of God's steadfast love as the basis for pardoning national sin; it is the inner reservoir of divine mercy sufficient to cover repeated failure.
God's steadfast love is shown to those who love and obey him across generations; it is a conditional-responsive divine loyalty, not a shame characteristic in this verse.

Steadfast love is tied to God's faithfulness and covenant-keeping across a thousand generations; it is a reliable inner commitment of the divine character directed toward those who reciprocate love and obedience.
God will maintain covenant and steadfast love as a response to Israel's obedience; the term names a divine relational commitment that the will of God sustains in proportion to human faithfulness.
Rahab appeals to reciprocal kindness: because she acted loyally toward the spies, she requests the same back for her household; steadfast love here is a moral debt owed by the will.
The spies pledge to deal kindly and faithfully with Rahab when the land is taken; loyal kindness is a vow that binds the will to future protective action in return for present help.
The spies promise to deal kindly with the man who helps them enter the city; loyal kindness is offered as a bargained commitment, engaging the will in a pledge of future favor.
Israel's failure to show steadfast love to Gideon's family is a moral failure of will and memory; the absence of loyal return for past good reveals a hardened, ungrateful inner disposition.

The verse describes the people of Laish as living undisturbed and 'lacking nothing'—this context does not appear to carry humiliation or shame inner-being content; the term may not be active in this verse's meaning.
Naomi invokes God's steadfast love as a wished-for return for her daughters-in-law's loyal care of the dead and of her; loyal kindness is a reciprocal relational commitment God himself mirrors.
Boaz instructs his men not to reproach (humiliate) Ruth while she gleans; humiliation is here an act of social shaming directed against a vulnerable outsider, which Boaz explicitly prohibits.

Naomi blesses Boaz whose kindness has not abandoned the living or the dead; steadfast love is a persistent, death-transcending loyalty that reflects a person's and God's enduring relational commitment.

Boaz praises Ruth's last act of loyal kindness as greater than her first because she chose duty over personal romantic preference; steadfast love here is a self-denying act of will prioritizing obligation.

God brings low (abases) as a sovereign act of will; humbling is God's prerogative that strips status and exaltation from persons, operating on their external condition as an expression of divine authority over human standing.
The Kenites showed kindness to Israel at the Exodus; Saul credits this historical loyalty as a moral claim on his present goodwill, showing that loyal kindness creates binding relational memory.
Jonathan is asked to act with covenant-bound loyalty toward David; steadfast love here is a covenantal obligation of the will engaged by a sworn relationship, carrying life-or-death stakes.

Jonathan asks David to show him the steadfast love of the Lord while he lives; the phrase elevates loyal kindness to a divine standard, making it the highest form of relational faithfulness expected between friends.

Jonathan asks that steadfast love not be cut off from his house even after he dies; loyal kindness is portrayed as a commitment that should outlast the person who initiated it, binding future will and action.

Saul declares Jonathan's choice brings shame on himself and his mother; shame here is the social and relational disgrace that falls on a person through dishonoring family loyalty and aligning with a perceived enemy.

Jonathan's grief and anger arise because his father had disgraced David; humiliation here produces intense emotional pain in a third party who witnesses it, showing that public disgrace damages the inner life of those who care for the shamed person.

David's men protected Nabal's shepherds from harm; the term (not causing humiliation) is evidenced by the absence of any mistreatment—restraint from shaming others reflects respectful inner regard for their dignity.

The shepherds confirm David's men treated them well and caused no harm or humiliation; the absence of reproach reflects an inner disposition of respect toward others even in situations of vulnerability.
David honors the men of Jabesh-gilead for showing loyal kindness to Saul in burial; the term names a faithful, honoring act toward the dead that merits divine blessing as a response.
David calls on the Lord to reciprocate steadfast love and faithfulness to those who showed it; loyal kindness generates a relational cycle where human faithfulness calls forth divine favor.
Abner defends himself by asserting he has continually shown steadfast love to Saul's house despite provocation; the term is used to claim moral credit for sustained loyalty under unjust accusation.

God's steadfast love will not be withdrawn from David as it was from Saul; this contrasts two destinies and shows that God's inner commitment of loyal love, once pledged, becomes an unconditional, permanent bond.

David seeks to show kindness to Saul's descendants for Jonathan's sake; loyal love here is driven by covenant memory, engaging David's will to honor a past friendship through acts toward surviving kin.

David seeks to show 'the kindness of God'—the highest form of loyal love—to Jonathan's crippled son; the phrase elevates the act to a divine standard, showing that this kindness mirrors God's own character.

David pledges kindness to Mephibosheth for Jonathan's sake, restoring land and table-fellowship; loyal love here is practically expressed through restoration of dignity and belonging to one who might otherwise have been an outcast.

David resolves to show loyal kindness to Hanun in return for his father's loyalty to him; steadfast love is presented as a relational obligation of the will grounded in reciprocity and covenant memory.
The men's humiliation is visible and social, felt so acutely in their bodies (shaved beards) that they cannot return to public life until the outward sign of disgrace has grown back.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense of steadfast loyalty and love; it does not evidence shame or disgrace as an inner-being state.
This verse uses che.sed in the sense of loyalty to a friend; it does not evidence shame or disgrace as an inner-being characteristic.
Humiliation drives inward behavioral retreat — the people sneak back into the city like those ashamed of fleeing battle, the inner shame reshaping their very posture and movement.

David's grief has covered his servants' faces with shame, inverting the inner experience of those who sacrificed for him — their joy of victory replaced by the inner wound of feeling dishonored by their king.
The verse presents cha.sad in its positive relational sense — God mirrors the merciful person's character — but does not directly evidence shame as an inner-being state here.
God abases the haughty by turning his eyes upon them in judgment, making abasement an act directed from outside onto the proud inner disposition of self-exaltation.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense of God's enduring covenant love toward David; no shame or disgrace inner-being content is present.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense of loyal kindness repaid to faithful supporters; no shame or disgrace inner-being content is evidenced.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense of God's great and steadfast love shown to David for his faithful heart; no shame inner-being content is present.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive covenantal sense — God's steadfast love given to those who walk wholeheartedly before him; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense — the kings of Israel are reputed to be merciful; no shame or disgrace inner-being content is evidenced.
Shame here is a social pressure felt inwardly by Elisha — he yielded to the urging of others 'until he was ashamed,' showing shame operating through conscience and relational will.
The prolonged stare produces an inner embarrassment or shame in Hazael — the gaze penetrates, producing a felt inner discomfort that precedes the prophet's weeping over what he sees.
The shame and confusion of defeated peoples is likened to withered grass — a total inner collapse of strength and confidence, leaving them helpless and disgraced before their conquerors.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense of God's eternal steadfast love as a reason for thanksgiving; no shame inner-being content is present.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense of God's enduring steadfast love celebrated in worship; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense — God pledges not to withdraw his covenant love from Solomon as he did from Saul; no shame inner-being content is present.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive relational sense — David reciprocates the kindness Nahash showed him; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.
The envoys' humiliation is bodily and social — their shaved beards make them too ashamed to reenter public life, so the king's compassion gives them space to recover before returning.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense of God's great steadfast love shown to David; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive doxological sense — God's enduring goodness and steadfast love proclaimed in corporate worship; no shame inner-being content is present.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive covenantal sense of God's steadfast love to those who walk before him with all their heart; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense — Solomon asks God to remember his covenant love for David; no shame inner-being content is present.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive doxological sense — the people bow in worship declaring God's eternal steadfast love; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive liturgical sense — God's enduring steadfast love is the content of the Levites' perpetual praise; no shame inner-being content is present.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense — God's steadfast love is the song sung before the army going into battle; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense — Joash failed to remember the kindness shown him by Jehoiada; the moral failure is ingratitude, not shame as an inner-being state.
The priests' humiliation was inner and conscience-driven — their own failure to be ritually prepared shamed them, and that inner shame moved them to immediate consecration and renewed service.
Sennacherib's shame is public and total — he returns to his homeland with face-shame after military catastrophe, and his disgrace follows him into his own temple where his sons kill him.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive doxological sense — God's steadfast love toward Israel is the ground of joyful praise at the temple's foundation; no shame inner-being content is present.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense — God's steadfast love extended through royal favor gave Ezra courage; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.
Ezra's shame is conscience-driven and anticipatory — he had publicly declared God's sufficiency, so asking for a military escort would expose inner inconsistency between his words and his trust.
Shame here is deeply inward — Ezra cannot lift his face toward God because guilt-consciousness fills him, the weight of corporate sin producing a felt inability to approach the holy.

Humiliation here is experienced as a physical and spiritual bowing under guilt — Ezra blushes and cannot lift his face, the inner collapse of dignity before God caused by accumulated communal iniquity.
Shame is presented as the culminating historical consequence of Israel's guilt — generations of sin have resulted in defeat, captivity, and utter public disgrace, visible to all nations to this day.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense — God's steadfast love extended to slaves in exile, providing reviving and protection; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive covenantal sense — God keeps steadfast love with those who love and obey him; no shame inner-being content is present.

This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense — God's abounding steadfast love stood in contrast to Israel's rebellion, preventing their complete abandonment; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive covenantal sense — God keeps steadfast love even amid the nation's long hardship; no shame inner-being content is present.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense — Nehemiah's good deeds are what he asks God to remember and not wipe out; the term here lacks shame inner-being content.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense — Nehemiah appeals to the greatness of God's steadfast love to spare him; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.

This verse uses che.sed in its positive relational sense — withholding kindness from a suffering friend is a moral failure that abandons the fear of God; no shame inner-being content is directly evidenced.
Shame arises from disappointed confidence — those who traveled hoping to find water find nothing, and the inner state of expectation collapses into the felt shame of being let down.
The shame of disappointment follows the collapse of confident hope — those who arrived expecting relief are humiliated by finding none, their trust publicly shown to have been unfounded.

Shame is presented as the fitting outer garment of God's enemies — those who hate the righteous will be clothed in disgrace, shame becoming a visible marker of inner moral failure and divine judgment.
This verse uses che.sed in its positive sense — God's steadfast love and care have preserved Job's life and spirit; no shame inner-being content is evidenced.
Disgrace fills Job's inner being so completely that he cannot lift his head — whether guilty or innocent, the felt weight of dishonor collapses his posture and self-perception before God.

Zophar challenges whether Job's boastful speech should go unchecked — he implies that Job deserves to be shamed publicly for his mocking, humiliation being the proper social response to arrogant talk.

Job challenges his friends' lack of shame in wronging him repeatedly — he appeals to their conscience, implying that continued reproach without cause should produce inner shame in those who inflict it.
Job accuses his friends of repeatedly wronging him without shame—the humiliation is an ongoing inner wound inflicted by others who feel no compunction about causing it.
Zophar feels the sting of censure as a personal insult that provokes an inner response—his understanding stirs a spirit within him to answer, so shame here triggers an inward, reactive indignation.
Abasement is the inner consequence of pride—when a person is brought low, it reflects the humbling of an arrogant disposition, while God lifts the lowly whose inner orientation is humble.
God commands the proud to be abased—abasement here is the crushing of inner pride by divine force, directed outward from God's anger toward every self-exalting person.
Honor belonging to the psalmist's inner person is being actively exchanged for shame by those who love falsehood—the shame is an ongoing reversal of his dignity caused by others' deceit.
This verse does not evidence shame content; it uses che.sed in its primary sense of God's steadfast love as the basis for the psalmist's access to God's house with reverence.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed here refers to God's steadfast love as the ground for the psalmist's plea for deliverance from distress.
The psalmist's enemies will be overtaken by shame and inner turmoil simultaneously—their confident opposition will collapse into sudden, disorienting disgrace as they are repulsed.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed here denotes God's steadfast love as the object of the psalmist's trust, which produces inner rejoicing rather than shame.
Shame is used as an instrument of oppression—the wicked try to shame the plans and inner confidence of the poor, but God as refuge counters that intended humiliation.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed here describes God's steadfast love as the source of wondrous salvation for those who take refuge in him.
The verb cha.sad here describes reciprocal faithfulness—God mirrors the inner disposition of the merciful person by showing mercy in return, reflecting an inner quality of loyal devotion.

God's abasing action is directed at haughty eyes—the proud inner disposition expressed through arrogant looks is brought down, while the humble are saved, showing abasement as divine correction of pride.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed here refers to God's enduring steadfast love shown to his anointed king and his descendants across generations.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed denotes the steadfast love of the Most High as the source of the king's inner stability and unshakeable security.
Trust in God is presented as the inner disposition that protects against shame—the ancestors cried out, trusted, and were not put to shame, so shame is averted by reliance on God.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed here denotes God's mercy and goodness as a constant accompanying presence throughout the psalmist's life.
The psalmist pleads against shame as an outcome of trust betrayed—if God fails to act, enemies will triumph and the psalmist's inner confidence in God will be publicly disgraced.
Shame is the fitting inner consequence for the treacherous—those who wait on God are protected from it, while wanton betrayers bear it as the just result of their inner corruption.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed refers to God's steadfast love as an enduring divine attribute for which the psalmist asks God to remember.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed here is the basis on which the psalmist appeals for God to remember him with grace rather than with judgment for past sins.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed describes all of God's ways as characterized by steadfast love and faithfulness toward covenant-keepers.
The psalmist asks God to guard his soul—shame here is an inner threat to the one who takes refuge in God, and refuge is the inner posture that shields against public disgrace.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed is presented as the constant inward focus of the psalmist's eyes, shaping his faithful walk.
Innocence is an inner moral condition expressed through the ritual washing of hands—the psalmist claims a clean conscience as the basis for his approach to God's altar in worship.

The term qe.ha.von (bluntness/innocence) in this context overlaps with niq.qa.von—both point to an inner moral integrity that the psalmist claims as he approaches God's altar. The verse does not evidence a distinct bluntness sense.
Taking refuge in God is the inner act that the psalmist pleads will protect him from shame—shame would mean that trust was misplaced; God's righteousness is the guarantee against it.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed is the object of the psalmist's rejoicing because God has seen his affliction and distress of soul, expressing grateful relief.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed here is the channel through which the psalmist appeals for salvation—God's steadfast love is the basis for rescue.
The psalmist distinguishes his own inner plea—calling on God—from the wicked who deserve shame; his calling is what shields him while the wicked face silence and Sheol.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed describes God's wonderfully shown steadfast love as the source of blessing experienced even in extreme crisis.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed here describes steadfast love as surrounding the one who trusts in God, contrasting inward security with the sorrows of the wicked.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed characterizes God's love as filling the entire earth, paired with his delight in righteousness and justice.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed is the object of the hope of those who fear God—their inner hope is directed toward his steadfast love.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed is a petition—the psalmist asks that God's steadfast love rest upon those whose inner orientation is hope in him.
Looking to God produces radiance—those who orient themselves toward him never bear the inward or outward collapse of the face that shame brings; their countenance is transformed.
The psalmist calls for shame and dishonor to fall on those who plot against his life—shame is the appropriate inner and social consequence for those who pursue evil against the innocent.
Disappointment and being turned back parallel shame here—those who devise evil should experience the inner collapse of their schemes and be driven away from their purpose.
Humiliation is called down on those who seek the psalmist's life—it is the social and inner degradation that should befall those who pursue unjust violence, as the just reversal of their aggression.

Those who gloat over the psalmist's calamity are called to be clothed with shame—shame here is a total inner and outward covering that overtakes those whose inner disposition is malicious self-magnification.
Shame is pictured as a garment that covers those who rejoice in another's suffering—their arrogant inner stance of self-exaltation is to be wrapped in disgrace as fitting punishment.
Being disappointed and turned back describes the inner and directional collapse of those who plotted against the psalmist—their confident schemes fail, producing inner defeat alongside shame.
Shame (ke.lim.mah) is the dishonor that clothes those who magnify themselves against the psalmist—it exposes their arrogance as the inner cause of the disgrace they must wear.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed describes God's steadfast love as immeasurably vast, reaching to the heavens, paired with his faithfulness that extends to the clouds.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed here is described as precious and is the reason humanity takes refuge in God—it is a quality that draws the inner person to shelter in him.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed is petitioned to continue flowing to those who know God, paired with righteousness for the upright of heart.
The righteous are not put to shame even in evil times—their inner security is unaffected by external crisis because their trust is well-placed, and shame (as inner collapse) does not overtake them.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed here is something the psalmist has publicly declared rather than hidden within his heart, showing it as a quality of God actively proclaimed.
This verse does not evidence shame content; che.sed and faithfulness are paired as the ongoing preserving forces the psalmist trusts God will not withhold from him.
Those who seek to destroy the psalmist are called to be covered with shame and dishonor—their malicious inner delight in his hurt is to be turned back and replaced with disgrace.
The psalmist calls for enemies who actively seek his destruction to experience shame and disappointment — the inward collapse of their hostile purpose frustrated and reversed against them.
Humiliation is invoked upon those who delight in the psalmist's hurt — their inner pleasure in his pain is met by being turned back and brought to dishonor, an outer defeat matching inward malice.
The mockers' own shame is to recoil upon them and appall them — those who taunted with 'Aha, Aha' are to be struck inwardly aghast by the disgrace their mockery brings back on themselves.
This verse uses che.sed in its primary sense of steadfast love, not shame; no inner-being shame content is present here — God commands his covenant love by day and gives the psalmist a song by night.
God's act of putting enemies to shame is celebrated as a reversal of their inner hostility — those who hate Israel are made to bear the disgrace that exposes their enmity as futile.
Israel experiences humiliation as something God has inflicted — being disgraced without divine military accompaniment leaves the people stripped of honor and exposed before their foes.
Shame is a constant visible covering over the psalmist's face — it is an all-day, inescapable inner-and-outer state of disgrace that cannot be hidden or shaken off.
Disgrace (ke.lim.mah) stands before the speaker perpetually — it is an exposed, humiliating condition that is 'before me' as an ongoing inner reality he cannot escape all day long.

The psalmist fears that his own condition might cause shame to fall on fellow seekers of God — being put to shame through another's failure would wound the inner hope of those who trust in the Lord.

Humiliation that could spread to God's seekers through the psalmist's plight is what he prays to be prevented — dishonor born through association would damage those whose inner orientation is toward God.

Dishonor covers the psalmist's face specifically because he bore reproach for God's sake — the shame is an inward and outward wound suffered vicariously, its cause being loyalty to God rather than personal failure.

Shame is one of three related experiences — reproach, shame, dishonor — that God fully knows; the psalmist's inner state of disgrace is laid bare before God as part of his suffering at the hands of foes.
Dishonor (ke.lim.mah) is named alongside reproach and shame as something God knows intimately — it marks the psalmist's inner humiliation before enemies, witnessed and held by God.
The psalmist invokes shame and confusion upon those who seek his life — their active hostility is to be met by an inward collapse of purpose, exposed and turned back upon themselves.
Confusion and shame are paired as the desired inner state of those who hunt the psalmist — being put to shame means their aggressive pursuit ends in frustrated disgrace rather than triumph.
Humiliation is called down on those who delight in the psalmist's hurt — being turned back and brought to dishonor is the fitting reversal for those whose inner delight was his suffering.
The mockers' own shame is to drive them back — those who jeered with 'Aha, Aha' are to turn back because of the shame that overtakes them, their taunting recoiling as inner disgrace.
Taking refuge in the Lord is the direct basis for the plea never to be put to shame — the psalmist's inner trust in God is what he appeals to as protection against the disgrace of being abandoned.
Shame and consumption are invoked together upon accusers — being covered with scorn and disgrace is the appropriate undoing of those whose inner aim was to do the psalmist harm.
Disgrace (ke.lim.mah) is what the psalmist prays will cover his accusers — it is the fitting inner and social humiliation for those who sought his hurt, countering their hostile intent.
The psalmist's tongue praises God's righteous help precisely because those who sought to hurt him have been put to shame and disappointed — their disgrace confirms God's vindication of the psalmist.
Those who sought to do the psalmist harm have been put to shame and left disappointed — their purpose has collapsed inwardly, and this failure is itself the content of the psalmist's praise.
The psalmist kept his heart clean and his hands in innocence — but this inner moral purity now seems futile, suggesting that innocence as an inner disposition brought no visible reward or protection.

The term qe.ha.von appears to overlap with niq.qa.von in this verse, both pointing to the maintained inner purity of hands and heart that the psalmist now doubts was worthwhile; no distinct shame content is present here.

The downtrodden and needy are at risk of turning back in humiliation — the prayer is that they not be left in shame but instead find cause to praise God's name, so their inner distress is addressed by divine intervention.

God alone executes the judgment of putting down one person and lifting up another — abasement is not self-caused or socially determined but an act of divine will that lowers the inner and outward standing of a person.

Shame is invoked as a means to a redemptive end — filling enemies' faces with dishonor is intended not merely as punishment but to drive them inwardly to seek the Lord's name.
The psalmist prays that enemies be put to shame and dismayed permanently — their inner and outward disgrace is to be total and final, ending in their perishing rather than recovery.
Perishing in disgrace is the ultimate fate called upon Israel's enemies — shame here is not merely social humiliation but an end-state, the final collapse of those who opposed God's people.

Shame from nations is an ongoing inner burden God vows to lift: Israel will no longer hear reproach or bear the disgrace of peoples, freeing the nation from the humiliation that caused it to stumble.

Shame is commanded as an appropriate inner response to grace: since God acts not for Israel's sake, the people are told to be ashamed and confounded for their own ways, linking shame to moral self-awareness before God's unmerited action.
Humiliation is the fitting inner posture before divine grace: the house of Israel is called to be confounded for its ways precisely because God acts on their behalf without their deserving it.

Shame will be forgotten in restored security: Israel will no longer remember past disgrace and treachery when they dwell safely, the inner wound of shame dissolved by God's permanent re-establishment of the people.

Humiliation is the intended inner effect of confronting the temple's design: seeing the temple's perfection is meant to produce in Israel an ashamed inner response to their own iniquities, a conscience-driven recognition of failure.

Shame becomes the gateway to instruction: only if Israel is ashamed of all they have done will the full design of the temple be disclosed to them, making humiliation before past sin the precondition for renewed knowledge of God's order.

Shame is a carried penalty restricting access: the Levites who committed abominations are barred from priestly nearness to God and must bear their shame as an ongoing inner-social consequence of their past deeds.
The term glossed 'shame' here references steadfast love (chesed); no shame content is present — the verse addresses God as the one who keeps covenant and loyal love with those who love and obey him.

Shame is the collective inner condition of Israel — publicly visible, contrasted with God's righteousness — resulting from treachery and covenant betrayal, and shared across all dispersed communities.
Shame belongs to the whole community — kings, princes, and ancestors — as the just inner and outward consequence of sin against God, owned corporately in confession.
Acting shamefully describes the mother's inner orientation — a willing, deliberate pursuit of lovers for material provision, revealing a conscience unrestrained by proper relational loyalty.
This verse does not use che.sed as shame; it presents steadfast love as part of God's betrothal covenant, an inner disposition of faithfulness and mercy directed toward Israel, not a shame concept.
The absence of steadfast love in the land reflects an inner communal failure — the people lack the heart-orientation of loyal covenant devotion toward God and one another.
God will convert their inner glory — their honored status — into dishonor as direct consequence of multiplied sin, making shame the inward and social reversal of their self-exaltation.
The rulers' deep affection for dishonor reveals a perverted will — they love what disgraces them, showing shame has become an object of desire rather than something to be avoided.
Shame will come upon them because of their sacrifices — their cultic acts, far from bringing honor, produce disgrace, felt as an inward humiliation tied to false worship.
Israel's love — its inner devotion and loyal affection — is transient and insubstantial, evaporating like morning mist, revealing a will unable to sustain genuine covenant faithfulness.
God desires steadfast love as an inner disposition of the heart more than ritual sacrifice, placing relational loyalty to him above external religious performance.
Innocence here names a moral-spiritual capacity the people are incapable of — God asks how long they will remain unable to attain purity of inner state, given their persistent idolatry.

The verse does not explicitly use 'bluntness' as a distinct term in the ESV text; the question of how long they are incapable of innocence implies a dulled moral sensitivity, but the term's inner-being content is minimal here.

Israel consecrated itself to a shameful idol at Baal-peor — shame describes the degrading inner transformation as they became like the detestable object of their devotion, replacing God with disgrace.
Israel's shame comes from its idol being carried off to Assyria — the people will experience inner humiliation as the object of their misplaced trust fails and is exposed as powerless.
Ephraim's shame is the public disgrace of having its cherished idol taken as tribute — an inner humiliation produced by the exposure of false trust placed in a powerless object.
Steadfast love is the harvest of righteous inner sowing — the verse frames it as a moral-spiritual yield awaiting those who cultivate right inner dispositions and seek God.
Holding fast to love is presented as a volitional inner act — the people are commanded to grasp loyal covenant devotion as the core orientation of their return to God.
Farmers are called to be ashamed at the destruction of the harvest — shame here is an appropriate inner response of grief and humiliation before catastrophic agricultural loss.
God's abounding steadfast love is the inner ground of his willingness to relent from disaster — it motivates his merciful response when the people return with hearts genuinely torn.
God's promise that his people will never again be put to shame indicates future inner restoration — disgrace will be permanently removed as God demonstrates his faithful provision.
God's presence among Israel as its Lord guarantees the people will never again be shamed — the inner security of knowing God is with them removes the possibility of future humiliation.
Shame will cover Edom as a consequence of its violence against Jacob — the image of shame as a covering describes it as an enveloping inner and public condition that results in permanent cutting off.
Those who cling to idols forfeit their hope of steadfast love — idolatry empties the inner life of its access to God's loyal devotion, exchanging real relational security for futile trust.

Jonah confesses that God's abounding steadfast love — his deep inner disposition to show mercy — was what he feared would lead God to spare Nineveh, revealing love as a defining divine character trait.
The inhabitants of Shaphir are told to pass in nakedness and shame — shame is the exposed, stripped inner and physical condition of a people under divine judgment, bereft of any covering dignity.
False prophets insist disgrace will not overtake them — they deny that the inner and communal shame of judgment could ever reach them, revealing a hardened conscience dismissing moral accountability.
Seers will be disgraced — their public shame is an inward humiliation caused by God's silence, as their prophetic claim is exposed as hollow when no divine answer comes.
The diviners are put to shame because God gives no answer — their shame is the inner humiliation of exposed incompetence, as silence from God collapses their professional and spiritual identity.
Loving kindness is presented as a core inner requirement God demands of humans — it is listed alongside justice and humility as a defining disposition of the inner moral life.
Shame will cover the enemy who mocked Israel's God — it is depicted as an enveloping inner and visible state that silences the mocker when God vindicates his people.
Nations will be ashamed of their military might upon seeing God's acts for Israel — shame comes as an inner collapse of confidence when their power is exposed as nothing before God.
God delights in steadfast love — it is his inner delight and defining characteristic that moves him to pardon iniquity rather than retain anger, making love the motivating core of his forgiveness.
God's steadfast love shown to Abraham is a sworn, faithfully kept commitment — it is an inner disposition of loyal devotion directed toward covenant people across generations.
God will expose Nineveh's nakedness before nations — shame here is the public inner humiliation of a proud city stripped of dignity and held up to contempt by divine judgment.
The oppressor has devised shame for his own house — his violent ambition produces inner disgrace that rebounds onto himself and his household, forfeiting his very life.
Utter disgrace will overwhelm the proud nation's glory — filling oneself with shame instead of honor describes the complete inner reversal of expected status as divine judgment falls.
Utter shame will come upon the proud oppressor's glory — dishonor is the inward and outward condition that God's cup of judgment brings, replacing exaltation with complete degradation.
The unjust person knows no shame — the absence of inner shame characterizes the wicked, who remain untouched by moral sensitivity despite God's daily visible righteousness before them.
God promises his people will not be put to shame on that day — inner disgrace over past rebellion will be removed, replaced by humility as the proud are taken away and shame is lifted.
God will change the shame of the outcast and lame into praise — shame as the inner burden of the humiliated and rejected will be transformed into honor and renown by divine restoration.
Showing kindness to one another is commanded alongside true judgment — steadfast love is an active inner orientation expressed in interpersonal mercy required by God in communal life.
Ekron's hopes are confounded — shame is implicit as the city's confident expectations collapse, producing inner dismay, though the verse centers on fear and anguish rather than explicit shame.
Israel's warriors will put the enemy horsemen to shame — defeating them produces their inner humiliation, as the power they trusted is overwhelmed by those fighting with the Lord's presence.

Every prophet will be ashamed of his vision — inner shame before false prophecy causes them to abandon their deceptive identity markers, revealing shame as an inward corrective force exposing pretense.
The father expects his son's presence will cause the tenants to feel respect — the inner movement of shame or reverence is the anticipated response to the son's authority and dignity.
The man without a wedding garment is rendered speechless — he has no inner resource of justification, his silence expressing the inward collapse of self-defense before an unanswerable challenge.
Jesus silenced the Sadducees — their voice is stopped by the force of his argument, leaving them with no inner recourse, their challenge publicly shut down before an audience.
Jesus commands the unclean spirit to be silent — the muzzling is an act of authority suppressing the spirit's voice, forcing inner cessation of its expression and compelling it to leave.

Jesus commands the sea to be still — the muzzling applies to the storm itself, imposing silence and calm by sheer authority, demonstrating power over forces that ordinarily have no inner will to restrain.
Bartimaeus throws off his cloak and springs up — the discarding of his outer garment signals an inner decisive act of abandonment of his former beggar's identity in eager response to Jesus's call.
The servant sent by the owner is physically struck and treated shamefully, showing dishonor expressed through violent, contemptuous action against a person representing another's authority.
The owner expects the tenants will feel inner regard or respect for his beloved son, implying that causing shame involves the failure to hold another in due esteem.
Jesus commands the demon to be silent, externally suppressing its voice; this use of muzzling applies to a malevolent spirit force, not a human inner-being state directly.
Jesus' adversaries are put to shame before the crowd when his miraculous works vindicate him, exposing their opposition as groundless and publicly humiliating them.
Shame arises as an inward, socially felt emotion when a self-promoted guest is publicly demoted to the lowest place, experienced as he moves there in disgrace before all present.
The manager's shame is an inner inhibition—a self-conscious reluctance rooted in pride—that makes begging feel beneath him, constraining his available options.
The unjust judge neither fears God nor respects man, meaning he has no inner sense of regard or deference toward others that would motivate just or considerate behavior.
The judge acknowledges to himself that he lacks fear of God or respect for people, showing that this absence of inner regard is a recognized character trait driving his unjust delay.
Those who trust in their own righteousness treat others with contempt, showing that self-sufficiency of will produces a dismissive, devaluing attitude toward fellow persons.
Another servant is beaten and treated shamefully by the tenants, expressing active contempt and dishonor directed against the one bearing the owner's authority.
The vineyard owner hopes that sending his beloved son will evoke in the tenants a sense of respect that will finally constrain their violent behavior toward his representatives.
Herod and his soldiers treat Jesus with contempt and mock him, expressing a dismissive inner attitude that regards him as of no worth or account.
Jesus contrasts honoring the Father with his accusers dishonoring him, showing dishonor as a willful inner refusal to acknowledge the worth of another, here directed personally against Jesus.
Jesus was rejected by the builders—a willful inner dismissal of his worth—yet God overturned that judgment by making him the cornerstone, vindicating him against human contempt.
The apostles rejoice at being counted worthy to suffer dishonor for Christ's name, showing that outward shame received for his sake is reframed inwardly as a mark of honor.
The silence of the lamb before its shearer describes a posture of non-resistance; applied to Christ, it speaks of a willed inner stillness that withholds self-defense under unjust suffering.
Some became stubborn and spoke evil of the Way publicly, expressing a reviling inner attitude that combined unbelief with hostile verbal contempt directed at Paul's message.
God gives people over to dishonoring their own bodies, showing that dishonor here is an inner moral degradation acted out physically when the conscience is abandoned to sinful desire.
Dishonorable passions are the inner driving force behind exchanging natural relations for unnatural ones, showing dishonor as a corrupted desire operating at the level of felt appetite.
Men consumed with passion for one another commit shameless acts, receiving in themselves the due penalty—showing indecency as a bodily-enacted moral disorder rooted in disordered inner desire.
Boasting in the law while breaking it constitutes dishonoring God, showing a self-contradicting inner stance where pride in religious identity conceals actual contempt for the lawgiver.
Hope in God does not produce inward shame because God's love poured into the heart by the Spirit confirms the hope, removing any basis for inner disappointment or disgrace.
The potter's sovereign right to make one vessel for honor and another for dishonor shows that dishonor is an assigned standing or purpose, not an inner emotional state here.
Whoever believes in the stone laid in Zion will not be put to shame, meaning faith prevents the inner experience of disgrace or defeat that unbelief produces when confronting God's action.
Everyone who believes will not be put to shame, affirming that faith in Christ secures the inner person against the disgrace of disappointed hope before God.
Those who eat are warned not to despise those who abstain, showing that contempt is an inner attitude of dismissal toward a fellow believer whom God has accepted.
Despising a brother over food practices reflects an inward attitude of contempt that ignores both the brother's standing before God and the coming universal judgment.
God chooses the foolish to shame the wise, using the inner experience of humiliation to overturn self-confident pride and expose the inadequacy of human wisdom.
God chooses what is despised and of no account in the world to nullify things that are, showing that divine election deliberately overturns the world's inner judgments of worth.
Paul writes to admonish, not to induce shame, distinguishing between causing painful inner humiliation and fatherly correction motivated by affection for his children.
Paul refers to those least esteemed in the church, implying that inner contempt or low regard for certain members is being acted on by bringing disputes before outsiders instead.
Paul invokes shame as a moral awakener, directing it inwardly at the Corinthians' failure to produce anyone wise enough to settle disputes without resorting to pagan courts.
The command not to muzzle a working ox is cited to argue for a worker's right to benefit from his labor; the inner-being content for the term itself is minimal here, applied to animals.
A man who prays or prophesies with his head covered dishonors his head, showing that a symbolic bodily act can carry an inner meaning of disrespect toward divinely ordered authority.
A wife who prays or prophesies with her head uncovered dishonors her head, showing dishonor as conveyed through a bodily-symbolic act that signals disregard for relational order.
It is disgraceful for a wife to cut or shave her hair, showing that shameful is a social-moral judgment tied to violations of accepted norms that reflect inner propriety and order.
Nature teaches that long hair on a man is a disgrace, meaning dishonor here is a felt social-moral judgment grounded in created order rather than a private inner state.
Humiliating those who have nothing at the Lord's Supper involves shaming the poor before others, inflicting social disgrace on the vulnerable through callous disregard of their dignity.
Mute idols cannot speak or respond, contrasting with the living Spirit who gives real utterance; the inner-being content here is the idols' total absence of voice, will, or agency.
Speaking in the assembly is called shameful for a woman in this context, indicating that certain public speech was judged socially and morally indecent within that ordered community setting.
Paul directs shame at those who lack knowledge of God and continue sinning, using shame as an inward corrective—a moral prod meant to awaken conscience and produce change.
The body is sown in dishonor at death but raised in glory, showing dishonor as the degraded, weak state of mortal existence contrasted with the transformed dignity of resurrection.
Paul warns that no one should despise Timothy, guarding him against dismissive contempt that would devalue his ministry and undermine his standing among the believers.
Paul renounces shameful, underhanded ways, showing that shame here is the moral quality of hidden, deceptive conduct that violates conscience and cannot bear open scrutiny before God.
Paul and his colleagues endure both honor and dishonor externally, showing that dishonor is an outward social treatment received from others that does not alter their inner integrity.
Paul's boasting about the Corinthians to Titus proved true, so he was not put to shame—meaning his inward confidence was vindicated and he was spared the humiliation of being proven wrong.
Paul fears being humiliated before Macedonians if the Corinthians are unprepared, showing shame as a felt social disgrace arising from the gap between confident boasting and actual reality.
Paul asserts he will not be ashamed even if his boast about apostolic authority seems excessive, showing freedom from inner shame grounded in confidence that his authority is genuinely from God.
Critics dismiss Paul's bodily presence and speech as of no account, showing that contemptuous rejection of a person extends to devaluing both their physical presence and their words.
Paul speaks ironically of his own weakness as a kind of shame, using the language of dishonor to acknowledge what his opponents see as inadequacy before pivoting to his own bold boasting.

Despite Paul's physical weakness being a burden, the Galatians did not reject or despise him — their inner disposition was one of acceptance, receiving him as a divine messenger rather than treating him with contempt.
Certain deeds done in secret are so morally degraded that even speaking of them is shameful — the shame is tied to the conscience's recognition that such things should not be named, let alone done.

Paul's eager hope is that he will not experience shame before Christ — the inner faculty of hope directs his will away from shame and toward courageous honor of Christ in his body, whatever the outcome.

Paul warns against those who advocate physical mutilation of the flesh, dismissing it as the work of evildoers — the inner-being content is minimal; the term describes a harmful external practice being repudiated.

These people's minds are set on earthly things and they glory in what is actually their shame — their conscience is inverted, treating disgrace as honor, revealing a corrupted inner orientation away from God.
The Thessalonians are warned not to dismiss or despise prophetic utterances — the inner disposition of contemptuous rejection toward Spirit-given speech is what is prohibited here.

Social withdrawal from a disobedient person is prescribed so that the resulting isolation will produce shame within him — shame functions here as an inward, corrective response aimed at restoring obedience.
Younger widows are urged to marry and manage households so as to give opponents no occasion for slander — reviling is an external verbal attack on reputation that right conduct is meant to preempt.

The verse uses the image of muzzling an ox to argue that workers deserve payment — the muzzling metaphor here has no inner-being content; it functions as an analogy for fair treatment of laborers, not as a characteristic of shame or disgrace.

In a great household some vessels are for honorable and some for dishonorable use — dishonor here describes a status of diminished worth assigned to certain vessels, implying the inner person's moral condition determines their standing before God.
False teachers upset whole families for shameful financial gain — the shame is moral corruption of the will, teaching what ought not be taught driven by greed rather than truth.

Sound, blameless speech causes opponents to be put to shame because they find nothing evil to say — shame here is the inner experience of an adversary confronted by irreproachable conduct and left without grounds for accusation.
Those who fall away re-crucify the Son of God and expose him to public contempt — the disgrace is directed outward toward Christ, describing a willful inner betrayal that publicly dishonors him.

Believers are urged not to throw away their confidence, which carries a great reward — the inner faculty at stake is bold confidence before God; abandoning it is framed as a self-destructive act of the will.

Jesus endured the cross by despising its shame — the inner will consciously set aside the disgrace attached to crucifixion, motivated by the joy set before him, showing shame can be overcome by a greater inner object of hope.

We respected earthly fathers who disciplined us — the inner response here is reverential submission prompted by correction; the verse uses this as the basis for an even stronger submission to God, not primarily evidencing shame's content.
Showing favoritism to the rich while dishonoring the poor man is condemned — the inner-being content is a failure of moral perception and will, treating a person of worth as if they had none.

Whoever believes in the chosen cornerstone will not be put to shame — faith directs the inner person toward Christ and shields them from the inner devastation of shame, with trust as the protective faculty.

Doing good silences the ignorant talk of foolish people — right conduct stops the mouth of critics; the inner-being content is minimal, as the verse focuses on silencing external detractors through virtuous action.

Believers are called not to return reviling with reviling but to bless instead — the inner will is directed away from retaliatory verbal attack toward blessing, as the calling shapes the inner response to verbal abuse.

A good conscience sustains good behavior in Christ, so that slanderers are put to shame — shame falls on the accusers whose inner moral judgment is exposed as false when confronted with blameless conduct.

Suffering as a Christian should not produce shame in the believer but rather glorifying of God — the inner will is directed away from shame's inner collapse toward God-honoring response, recasting suffering as occasion for praise.

A normally speechless donkey rebuked Balaam's madness — the muteness of the animal is a literal physical condition used to contrast the prophet's irrationality; minimal inner-being content for the characteristic of shame or disgrace directly.

Abiding in Christ produces inner confidence at his appearing, so that believers do not shrink back in shame — shame here is the soul's recoil before Christ when unabiding; abiding is the inner protective stance against it.
False teachers cast up the foam of their own shame like wild waves — their shame is generated from within themselves, an outward eruption of inward moral degradation that they cannot contain.

The Laodicean church's spiritual poverty is likened to naked shame that must be covered by garments from Christ — shame here is the exposed inner condition of spiritual destitution, visible to God and needing divine remedy.

The one who stays awake and keeps his garments avoids being seen exposed and indecent — the indecency here is moral and spiritual unpreparedness made visible at Christ's coming, representing an inner failure of vigilance.
